coredata.dc:title: `Anionic surfactant-assisted the transport of carbon dots through saturated soil and its variation with aqueous chemistry
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiuyan
Xie, Lang
Zhang, Qiang
Wei, Qiqi
Farooq, Usman
Chen, Weifeng
Miao, Renhui
Qi, Zhichong
coredata.dc:description: There is little understanding of how anionic surfactants affect the transport of carbon dots (CDs) nanoparticles in soil. In this study, the mobility characteristics of CDs in soil columns with sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, a model anionic surfactant) under various conditions were investigated. The results showed that the presence of SDS enhanced CDs mobility at pH 7.0 when Na+ was the main background electrolyte. The increased electrostatic repulsion, the competitive deposition between SDS and nanoparticles for the sites on soil particle surfaces, the decreased straining effect, and the steric hindrance were all attributed to trend. Interestingly, in the presence of divalent metal cation (i.e., Ca2+ or Cu2+), SDS suppressed the mobility of CDs. This phenomenon was most likely stemmed from the precipitation of scum (i.e., SDS–Cu2+/Ca2+ complexes) and adsorbed SDS-metal-CDs complexes. Furthermore, the extent of the enhanced effects of SDS on CDs mobility decreased with increasing solution pH from 5.0 to 9.0, which was primarily due to a decrease in the steric effect and deposition site competition. A two-site transport model was also applied to fit the observed mobility data of CDs in soil media. Overall, findings from this work provide meaningful insights into the fate of co-existing carbon-based nanoparticles and anionic surfactants in the subsurface systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
SDS
Saturated soil
Two site transport model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhanced transport of CDs by SDS
Effect of SDS on the transport of CDs in the presence of divalent metal cation
Effect of SDS on the transport of CDs under different pH conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of SDS on the transport of CDs (in 10 mM NaCl at pH 7.0) in saturated soil columns (columns 1 −4, Table 1). Solid lines are plotted by curve-fitting experimental data with the two-site transport model.
Correlations between fitted parameters of two-site transport model (based on breakthrough data of columns 1–4) and SDS concentration.

Effects of SDS (10 mg/L) on the transport of CDs in saturated soil columns in the presence of divalent cations: (a) 0.5 mM Ca2+ (columns 5 and 6, Table 1); and (b) 0.5 mM Cu2+ (columns 7 and 8, Table 1). Solid lines are plotted by curve-fitting experimental data with the two-site transport model.

Effect of SDS (10 mg/L) on the transport of CDs at different pH conditions: (a) pH 5.0 (columns 9 and 10, Table 1); (b) pH 7.0 (columns 1 and 4, Table 1); and (c) pH 9.0 (columns 11 and 12, Table 1). Solid lines are plotted by curve-fitting experimental data with the two-site transport model.

The total masses of retained CDs with or without SDS under different pH conditions (ionic strength = 10 mM NaCl). The total mass of retained CDs in column = ((influent mass – effluent mass –eluted mass)/influent mass) × 100%.
Experimental setups and breakthrough results of column experiments.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average value of last three data points of respective breakthrough curve.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values after ± sign represent standard deviation of five replicates.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Hydrodynamic diameter (D h) of CDs nanoparticles based on dynamic light scattering analysis.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: d p/d c represents ratio of D h of CDs aggregates to average diameter of soil grains.

head.ce:title: `Anionic surfactant-assisted the transport of carbon dots through saturated soil and its variation with aqueous chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Humid Subtropical Eco-geographical Processes of the Ministry of Education/ Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory for Plant Eco-physiology/ School of Geographical Sciences, Fujian normal university, Fuzhou, Fujian 350007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is little understanding of how anionic surfactants affect the transport of carbon dots (CDs) nanoparticles in soil. In this study, the mobility characteristics of CDs in soil columns with sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, a model anionic surfactant) under various conditions were investigated. The results showed that the presence of SDS enhanced CDs mobility at pH 7.0 when Na+ was the main background electrolyte. The increased electrostatic repulsion, the competitive deposition between SDS and nanoparticles for the sites on soil particle surfaces, the decreased straining effect, and the steric hindrance were all attributed to trend. Interestingly, in the presence of divalent metal cation (i.e., Ca2+ or Cu2+), SDS suppressed the mobility of CDs. This phenomenon was most likely stemmed from the precipitation of scum (i.e., SDS–Cu2+/Ca2+ complexes) and adsorbed SDS-metal-CDs complexes. Furthermore, the extent of the enhanced effects of SDS on CDs mobility decreased with increasing solution pH from 5.0 to 9.0, which was primarily due to a decrease in the steric effect and deposition site competition. A two-site transport model was also applied to fit the observed mobility data of CDs in soil media. Overall, findings from this work provide meaningful insights into the fate of co-existing carbon-based nanoparticles and anionic surfactants in the subsurface systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emergent type of carbon containing nanomaterials [1], have piqued the interest of researchers since first reported in 2004 [2], owning to their unique physico-chemical properties, which include high-fluorescence quantum yields, chemical stability, an abundance of oxygen-containing groups, and electronic properties [1,3,4]. Generally, CDs is to be considered as a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with size less than 20 nm in diameter [5]. Due to wide raw materials (graphite, graphene oxide, small molecules, polymers, etc.) and multifarious synthesis methods (top-down and bottom-up) [6–8], a large family of CDs form up rapidly [5]. Several previous studies classified the CDs into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [5,9]. CDs exhibit great potential in various industries such as drug delivery [10,11], sensing metal ions [12], fuel cells [13,14], and bioimaging [15,16]. Undoubtedly, the increased use of this nanomaterial will inevitably result in its dissemination into the natural environment [17]. In this case, CDs may pose a potential threat to different environmental media (surface water, soil, and groundwater systems) [18]. Several recent studies have demonstrated that CDs may have a series of toxic and adverse effects on living organisms [4,19]. Understanding the deposition, transport, and fate of CDs is thus critical for their benign use and risk management [18].

CDs can be quite high mobile under various environmental conditions due to its good water dispersibility and amounts of functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, amide, and carboxyl groups) embedded within the structure of CDs [20]. Recently, merely a few systematic studies have been carried out to examine the transport and retention of CDs in environmental media (e.g., quartz sand) [20–23]. Kamrani et al. reported that the mobility behaviors of CDs in saturated sand could be affected by a number of water chemistry (e.g., solution pH and ionic strength) and physical factors (e.g., collector grain size) [20]. Moreover, CDs nanoparticles may facilitate contaminant (e.g., heavy metal ions) transport in subsurface environments because of their high affinity for heavy metal ions and high mobility [20,23]. Nevertheless, the influences of some other critical environmental factors (e.g., surfactant) on the mobility of CDs in porous media have yet to be investigated.

Surfactants are substances that reduce surface tension (or interfacial tension) between two surfaces (e.g., two liquids, a liquid and a solid, or a liquid and a gas) [24]. In general, they made up head and tails of hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups respectively [25]. Surfactants are classified as anionic, cationic, nonionic and amphoteric (zwitterionic) based on their polar head group [26]. Anionic surfactants are the most commonly used surfactants among these types of surfactants [26]. These compounds have become widely used materials in everyday life, such as detergents and health care, as well as industrial fields including biotechnology and pharmacy [25]. Consequently, considerable amounts of various anionic surfactants have recently been detected in the natural environment (e.g., household wastewater, industry wastewater, and natural surface water) [27,28]. Generally, the surfactant concentration in soil was at μg/kg level [29]. Nevertheless, it has been reported that the total concentration of detergents ranged from a few hundred mg/L to many g/L in industrial wastewater [30,31]. The long-term application of industrial wastewater containing high surfactant concentration could cause the accumulation of surfactants in soil–water system [31]. More importantly, anionic surfactants may adsorb onto the surfaces of porous media (e.g., soil and sand) and subsequently influence the physical and chemical characteristics of these media [31–33]. Meanwhile, previous studies confirmed that anionic surfactants can interact with carbon-based nanomaterials including graphene oxide (GO) [34–36], fullerene (C60) [37], and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [38–40] through multi-mechanisms (e.g., van der Waals force, H-bonding, and hydrophobic interaction). As a result, anionic surfactants are likely to influence CDs transport through aquifer media. Several studies in recent years have looked into the effects of anionic surfactants on the mobility and deposition of carbon-based nanomaterials like GO [41–44], C60 [45–47], and CNTs [40,46,48]. However, the transport characteristics of CDs with surfactants are still not reported in the literature. Therefore, both extensive and intensive study designs are ultimately necessary to comprehend the effect of anionic surfactants on CDs mobility under diverse environmental circumstances.

In this study, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was chosen as the model surfactant and soil was used as the model porous media. SDS's effect on CDs transport under various environmental conditions was investigated using a series of column experiments. The following are the main goals of this research: (1) to identify the mechanisms that control the transport of CDs when SDS is present, (2) to systematically examine the impact of SDS on CDs transport when divalent metal cations (i.e., Cu2+and Cu2+), (3) to investigate the effect of SDS on CDs mobility as affected by different solution pH values (i.e., 5.0, 7.0, and 9.0). Besides, additional sorption experiments were also carried out to illustrate the binding affinities of SDS to soil grains under a number of conditions. Furthermore, the two-site transport model was utilized to help elucidate the mobility and retention properties of engineered nanoparticles through soil media. These study findings may be beneficial in determining the transport characteristics of emerging carbon nanoparticles in the subsurface systems when they are combined with ubiquitous surfactants.

Surface soil (0–20 cm), collected from Puyang area (Henan province, China), was chosen as model porous media. Soil samples were taken to the laboratory, air-dried at room temperature, crushed and sieved < 2 mm prior to further use. The soil contained 32.6% sand, 62.1% silt and 5.3% clay with pH value of 7.56. The fractional organic carbon (f OC) value of the sample was 1.49% with average particle size of 112 µm. The particle size distribution (PSD) of soil grains was measured by the sieve-pipette method (SPM) (Detailed procedure is given in Section S1, Supplementary Material) [49]. The cation exchange capacity (CEC) and anion exchange capacity (AEC) are 9.80 cmol/kg and 0.72 cmol/kg. The CEC and AEC values of soil were determined according to the method described elsewhere [50,51]. The ζ-potential values of porous media under different conditions were measured using a Zetasizer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern Instruments, Ltd., Westborough MA) [52].
SDS (≥ 98%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The physicochemical properties of this surfactant were listed in Table S1.

CDs suspensions were prepared on the basis of the method described by Zhu et al. [53]. Detailed description of the procedure can be found in Supplementary Material (Section S2). The morphology of CDs was performed a JEM-2100 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Ltd., Japan). The TEM images showed that particle size distributions of the CDs ranged from 2 nm to 6 nm (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) were used to observe the surface functional groups of samples. The C content (47.3%) was performed on a Elementar Vario EL Cube elemental analyzer (Germany). Furthermore, the total organic carbon (TOC) content of the stock suspension was assayed with a TOC analyzer (model TOC-5050A, Shimadzu Scientific Instruments, Columbia, MD, USA). Consequently, the CDs concentration in the suspension was calculated (132 mg/L). The ζ-potential and hydrodynamic diameter (D h) of CDs suspensions was determined using a Zeta PALS instrument as mentioned before.

The effect of SDS (1–10 mg/L) on the mobility of CDs nanoparticles in saturated porous media was studied using soil column experiments. The concentrations of SDS used in this work were dependent on those in previous studies [31,41,43]. For each transport experiment, the soil particles were uniformly dry-packed into a borosilicate glass column (10.3 cm long and 1.6 cm diameter) step by step. The column porosity and density was found to be 0.52 ± 0.02 and 1.26 ± 0.02 g/cm3, respectively. The injection solutions (i.e., influents) were obtained by mixing CDs suspension, electrolyte ions (i.e., NaCl, CaCl2, or CuCl2), and SDS solution at room temperature ( Table 1). The pH values of each of the solutions were adjusted with 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH using a pH meter. Before the transport experiment, the soil column was saturated with 10 pore volumes (PV, ~100 mL) of deionized water (DI water) via an up flow mode into the column through a syringe pump manufactured by KD Scientific, Boston MA. All experiments were carried out under a pore-water velocity of 0.058 cm/min to substantially remove air pockets. Then, 10 PV of background electrolyte solution (Table 1) was pumped into the soil column to achieve steady state. Afterward, ~4 PV of 30 mg/L CDs solution with or without SDS was introduced into the soil column. Effluent samples (~3.5 mL) were collected at predetermined time intervals (~30 min). The absorbance of CDs in the effluent solutions was measured by a TU-1810PC UV–vis spectrophotometer at 335 nm (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). Then the concentrations of CDs were calculated according to a pre-established calibration curve (Fig. S2) [21]. Every experiment was repeated at least two times.

Sorption experiments were carried out to investigate the adsorption capacities of SDS by soil particles (i.e., the adsorption amount of SDS onto the soil (q (mg/g)) under the experimental conditions) [54]. More detailed descriptions of the adsorption procedures are given in Sections S3, Supplementary Material.

In this work, a two-site transport model that accounts for both attachment and straining was employed to analyze the obtained transport results of CDs in the soil columns[55–57]. In this model, the attachment rate (K att (h−1)), the maximum retention capacity of CDs on the attachment site (S max (mg/kg)), and the straining rate (K str (h−1)) were obtained by direct operational model fitting of the breakthrough curves of CDs [55]. Furthermore, by fitting of non-reactive tracer (KBr) curves based on CXTFIT model, the value for dispersion coefficient (D) was obtained (Fig. S3) [58]. Detailed equations are presented in Section S4 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced transport of CDs by SDS
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 clearly demonstrated that SDS could enhance CDs mobility under the experimental conditions (pH 7.0 in 10 mM NaCl). For example, the maximum breakthrough of CDs without SDS (as represented by the C/C 0 value) was 63.0%; in comparison, the maximum C/C 0 value increased to essentially 72.6% in the presence of 10 mg/L SDS (Table 1), indicating that less CDs was deposited in the soil column in the presence of SDS (Table S2). Interestingly, the transport-enhancement effects of SDS increased with increasing the SDS concentration from 1 mg/L to 10 mg/L (e. g., Fig. S4 showed that there was a linear correlation between the total masses of retained CDs and the SDS concentration), indicating that the enhanced effects were SDS-concentration dependent [59,60].

The enhanced effect of SDS on CDs transport was attributed to the following mechanisms, according to our hypothesis. To begin, it has been reported that SDS can be adsorbed by soil particles mainly through the hydrophobic mechanism (i.e., the interaction between the alkyl chains of SDS molecules and hydrophobic sites on the soil surfaces) [61–63]. Also, the binding of SDS to soil particles was evidenced by the adsorption amounts of the SDS onto soil particles in Table S3. Meanwhile, SDS binds to carbon nanoparticles through hydrophobic effect and hydrogen bonding interaction (the FTIR spectroscopy of CDs showed that CDs contained amounts of functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino)) (Fig. S5) [34,36]. Consequently, this anionic surfactant had a significant impact on the ζ-potential values of soil and CDs. That is, after the addition of SDS, the soil particles and CDs became more negatively charged (Table S4 and Fig. S6a). As a result, electrostatic repulsion between CDs and porous media was likely to contribute to facilitating transport of CDs [40,47]. Secondly, previous studies confirmed that straining effects significantly contributed to the deposition of colloids/nanoparticles when the ratios of diameters of colloids/nanoparticles to collector (i.e., porous media grains) (i.e., d p/d c (where d p and d c are average diameters of particle and collector, respectively)) is greater than 0.002–0.003 [55,64,65]. In this work, straining effect was likely to have contributed to the retention of CDs under experimental conditions (as indicated by the d p/d c values of CDs in Table 1). Notably, SDS, as a surfactant could promote the dispersion of nanoparticles. The particle sizes of CDs (Table 1 and Fig. S6b) demonstrated that the addition of SDS inhibited the aggregation of CDs. Similarly, several published works also reported that other carbonaceous nanomaterials (e.g., GO, C60, and CNTs) were well-dispersed with surfactants [35,37,39,40,66]. It is well known that reducing the size of colloids/nanoparticles is beneficial in suppressing the straining effect [21,46,65,67,68]. In this case, the smaller particle size is likely to have played important role in the enhanced effects of SDS on CDs mobility [21,60]. Thirdly, anionic surfactants are expected to adsorb on soil surfaces in a tail-on fashion because of the electrostatic repulsion between the negative charged soil particles and the head group of SDS. Meanwhile, soil grains contained appreciable amounts of clay minerals (~5.3%), thus anionic exchange was likely to contribute to the adsorption of SDS onto the clay fraction (the AEC value of the soil was 0.72 cmol/kg) [69–71]. Consequently, the binding of SDS to CDs and to soil could promote the steric hindrance between the nanoparticles and porous media, resulting in the enhanced transport of CDs [40,47]. Fourthly, SDS also masked soil surface chemical heterogeneity as SDS was deposited on the media surface, reducing the availability of retention sites [72,73]. That is, the competition of SDS molecules against CDs for the retention sites on soil surfaces was also another critical factor for CDs mobility [73]. In the end, previous studies confirmed that the presence of anion surfactants such as SDS could release soil colloids from the solid phase (via steric repulsion as well as the decrease of van der Waals attraction between colloids and solid surface [74,75]) and mobilize soil organic matter (through reducing the surface tension of organic matter [76]). Hence, the soil colloids and soil organic matter in aqueous phase were also likely to facilitate CDs mobility [45,75].

Furthermore, the transport data were fitted with a mathematical model (i.e., two-site transport model) in order to further elucidate the transport-enhancement effects of SDS on CDs. The fitting parameters (i.e., K att, S max, and K str) were shown in Table S5. Values of the parameters were plotted against the concentrations of SDS ( Fig. 2). The decrease of all the three parameters with increasing SDS concentrations was consistent with the mechanisms governing SDS’s enhanced effects on CDs mobility. The decrease in the K att values was mainly stemmed from the increase of the electrostatic repulsion between nanoparticles and soil surfaces, as well as the deposition site competition and the steric hindrance. Meanwhile, the decrease in the K str value could be ascribed to the decrease of D h values with increasing SDS concentrations (Table 1 and Fig. S6b). Moreover, the decreased trend of S max value indicated that part of attachment sites on the surfaces of soil were disappeared when the SDS was added [45]. As mentioned above, the masking of certain surface heterogeneity of soil grains eliminated the favorable deposition sites and suppressed the transport of engineering nanomaterials [31,41]. Furthermore, for a given SDS concentration, the K str for CDs was greater than K att (Fig. 2), indicating that the straining effects were more important role in controlling CDs transport through soil columns [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of SDS on the transport of CDs in the presence of divalent metal cation
ce:section.ce:para: Besides monovalent metal cations (e.g., Na+ and K+), several divalent metal ions including Mg2+, and Cu2+, Ca2+ are common in natural aqueous environments [77,78], which may influence the mobility of CDs. Hence, this work investigated the effect of SDS on the transport of CDs with divalent cations (using Ca2+ and Cu2+ as model cations), and the breakthrough curves are shown in Fig. 3. Interestingly, the influence of the anionic surfactant (i.e., SDS) on CDs mobility in the presence of Cu2+ was not comparable to that of Na+. When the solution contained divalent metal ions, however, SDS inhibited the CDs transport (e.g., in the presence of Cu2+, the retained mass of CDs increased from 64.3% (without SDS) to 66.8% (with 10 mg/L SDS)) as depicted in Table S2.

The inhibitory role of SDS in CDs mobility was ascribed to the following factors. On the one hand, due to electronegativity of oxygen over carbon atoms and abundant active functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, amide, and carboxyl groups, Fig. S5), CDs exhibit high affinity toward divalent metal ions via electrostatic attraction and complexation mechanisms [20,23,79]. In this case, divalent cations, carbon nanomaterials (e.g., CDs), and surfactants can form ternary complexes (i.e., CDs-metal-SDS complexes) [43,57,80]. Meanwhile, the formation of scum (i.e., the precipitation of SDS–Cu2+/Ca2+ complexes) was sufficiently adsorbed onto soil particle surfaces in the presence of divalent cation [81–84]. In this case, portion of SDS associated CDs were deposited on soil surfaces in the presence of Ca2+, resulting in suppressing CDs mobility. Furthermore, the optimal-fit K att value for CDs without SDS retention obtained from the two-site model also was lower than that with SDS (Table S5). On the other hand, considering that SDS could bind to soil grains [61–63]; therefore, the cation (Ca2+ or Cu2+) could serve as a bridge connection which linked the surface-adsorbed SDS and CDs nanoparticles (i.e., formed the SDS-metal-CDs complexes) [57]. Consequently, more nanoparticles were deposited onto the surfaces of soil grains.

Additionally, another interesting result was that CDs with or without SDS exhibited a considerable higher mobility in the presence of Ca2+ compared to Cu2+ (Fig. S7). As previously stated, CDs contained amounts of functional group (Fig. S5); more importantly, the compositions of soil (e.g., the groups existed on the clay particles, iron oxide particles, and soil organic matter) also had oxygen-containing functional groups [85]. As a result of cation bridge effects (i.e., Ca2+/Cu2+ acted as a bridging moiety between CDs and soil particles), divalent metal cations could promote CDs deposition on soil surfaces [43,86–88]. Meanwhile, Cu2+ had a stronger binding ability with soil/CDs than Ca2+ [89,90]. As a result, the inhibitory effect of Cu2+ on the transport of CDs was greater than that of Ca2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of SDS on the transport of CDs under different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Solution pH is one of critical factor affecting the transport and deposition behaviors of carbon nanoparticles because pH values strongly influence the surface charges of porous media and nanomaterials, as well as the aggregation characteristics of nanoparticles [91–93]. As a result, it is critical to explore the influence of SDS on the CDs transport properties under various pH conditions. An initial SDS concentration of 10 mg/L was used under three pH conditions (5.0, 7.0, and 9.0 in 10 mM NaCl). The presence of SDS enhanced CDs mobility under all experimental conditions, as shown by the breakthrough curves in Fig. 4. The main controlling mechanisms as revealed in Section 3.1, were the increase of electrostatic repulsion, a decrease in steric hindrance, and competition between SDS molecules and CDs for deposition sites on soil surfaces [40,47,64,72,73].

Interestingly, the enhanced effect of SDS on the transport of CDs decreased with increased in background solution pH values ( Fig. 5). For example, as shown in Table S2, the mass retained in column of CDs was reduced by about 10.7% (from 46.5% (without SDS) to 35.8% (with 10 mg/L SDS)), 7.2% (from 40.2% to 33.0%), and 0.2% (from 23.2% to 23.0%) at 5.0, 7.0, and 9.0, respectively. Note that the soil surfaces became more negatively charged as increased pH values. As a result, the adsorption capacity of SDS onto soil grains decreased with rising pH because of the increased electrostatic repulsion Table S3 [82], resulting in the decline of the competitive deposition effect and steric hindrance induced by the adsorbed SDS. Hence, the extent of the enhanced effect was greater in acidic conditions than in alkaline conditions.

Furthermore, regardless of SDS-free or SDS-addition, CDs mobility increased as solution pH increased values (Fig. S8). The phenomenon was primarily due to the increase of electrostatic repulsion between CDs nanoparticles and soil particles (as evidenced by the changes in the ζ-potential values of nanoparticles and soil in Table 1 and S4) and the decrease of the aggregate size of CDs (Table 1) [21,40,47]. This mechanism was well demonstrated by trends of K att and K str values obtained from the transport model, which can be found in Table S5 and Fig. S9.

In this work, steady-flow soil column experiments under saturated conditions were used to study the mobility behaviors of CDs in the presence of anionic surfactants (i.e., SDS). The data presented above clearly showed that SDS facilitated CDs transport when Na+ was the predominant background electrolyte (pH 7.0). This observation was mainly caused by the increased electrostatic repulsion, the deposition site competition, the decreased straining effect, and the steric hindrance induced by adsorbed SDS molecules. Contrarily, when solution contained divalent metal cations (e.g., Ca2+ or Cu2+), SDS inhibited CDs transport due to the precipitation of SDS–Cu2+/Ca2+ complexes onto soil surfaces as well as the formation of adsorbed SDS-metal-CDs complexes. Interestingly, the extent of the transport-enhancement effects of SDS decreased with increasing solution pH from 5.0 to 9.0, which was attributed primarily to the decline of the steric effect and retention site competition with increasing pH. Moreover, the two-site transport model may be effective in understanding the deposition mechanisms for the transport of CDs with or without surfactant through soil columns. In summary, these findings confirmed that anionic surfactants play critical roles in controlling the fate of carbon-based nanoparticles in subsurface systems under variety of environmental conditions.

Jiuyan Chen: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lang Xie: Investigation, Software, Qiang Zhang: Investigation, Qiqi Wei: Validation. Usman Farooq: Investigation, Weifeng Chen: Conceptualization, Renhui Miao: Writing – review & editing. Zhichong Qi: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the NSFC-Shandong United Fund (U1906222), the National Key Research And Development Program (2019YFC1804104), Henan University post-doctoral start-up research funds (CJ3050A0670001), the Project Management of Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for Minsheng College Students (KCCXSY-2021-059), and Chinese Scholarship Council (201708420145).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128860.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photochemical transformations of carbon dots in aqueous environments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.P. Frank, L.R. Sigmon, A.R. Deline, R.S. Lankone, M.J. Gallagher, B. Zhi, C.L. Haynes, D.H. Fairbrother, Photochemical transformations of carbon dots in aqueous environments, Environ. Sci. Technol. 54 (2020) 4160–4170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, R. Ray, Y. Gu, H.J. Ploehn, L. Gearheart, K. Raker, W.A. Scrivens, Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 126 (2004), 12736–12737.
